name: Paroxysms

toc.text: Non‑Epileptic Paroxysmal Events of Early Childhood
BHS vs Seizure vs Cardiac Syncope

html:
Non‑Epileptic Paroxysmal Events of Early Childhood

Overview

"Funny" or "weird" spells are among the most common child neurology consults and are frequently nonepileptic. In infants and toddlers, the most common benign causes are primary motor stereotypies, shuddering attacks, and breath‑holding spells, but a broad range of non‑epileptic paroxysms can mimic seizures or syncope. Key questions: trigger, awareness, duration, pattern repeatability, and video review.

Primary Motor Stereotypies

Onset: infancy/early childhood (often 6–24 months).

Phenomenology: brief, repetitive, patterned movements (e.g., hand flapping, arm waving, head nodding, body rocking, leg kicking).

Typical features:

  • Last 1–2 seconds up to several seconds; often occur in clusters.
  • No alteration in awareness; child can be distracted or interrupted.
  • Occur during excitement, anticipation, engrossment (videos, music, feeding, high chair, car seat, activity center).
  • Same movement every time; non‑evolving pattern over months.
  • Can have dystonic character.

Course: often persist but may lessen; seen in typical development and in neurodevelopmental disorders.

Management: explanation and reassurance; consider brief developmental screening; no EEG, imaging, or ASM if classic.

Shuddering Attacks

Age: usually 4–12 months.

Phenomenology: sudden brief "shiver" or tremor of head/shoulders/trunk, often with facial grimace or arm stiffening; parents describe "like ice‑cold water poured down the neck."

Duration: 1–2 seconds; can recur many times daily.

Awareness: intact; child continues activity; no postictal state.

Triggers: excitement, feeding, frustration, or spontaneous.

Course: benign; typically improve with age.

Management: reassurance; no routine EEG or imaging when history and exam are typical.

Breath‑Holding Spells (BHS)

Breath‑holding spells are brief, non‑epileptic events in young children where emotion or pain triggers apnea, color change, and transient loss of consciousness with full recovery. Cyanotic spells follow crying/anger with forced expiration, cyanosis, and brief limpness or stiffening; pallid (reflex anoxic) spells follow sudden pain or fright with marked pallor and syncope‑like collapse. Spells usually start between 6–18 months and remit by early school age without long‑term neurologic sequelae. Mechanism is a brainstem/autonomic reflex (inspiratory arrest or vagally mediated asystole), often worsened by iron deficiency. Management is reassurance, safety positioning during spells, consider ECG once for arrhythmia/long QT, and treat iron deficiency; medications are rarely needed.

Quick Comparison: Breath‑Holding Spell vs Seizure vs Cardiac Syncope

Feature Breath‑holding spell Epileptic seizure Cardiac syncope / arrhythmia
Typical age6–18 months (rare >5 y)Any ageOften school‑age/teen
Usual triggerCrying, pain, frustrationOften none; sometimes fever, sleep loss, lightsExertion, startle, fright, standing; may be no prodrome
Color changeCyanotic or pallid at onsetCyanosis may occur laterPallor common; cyanosis if prolonged
SequenceCry → breath‑hold → color change → limp/± stiff, ± brief jerks → rapid recoverySudden LOC → tonic/clonic movements → postictal confusionSudden collapse → brief LOC ± myoclonic jerks → rapid reorientation if rhythm recovers
DurationUsually <30–60 sTypically 1–2 minSeconds to <1 min (unless persistent arrhythmia)
Post‑eventImmediate or rapid baseline; no postictalPostictal confusion, fatigue, headacheRapid recovery; may feel light‑headed
Work‑upHistory/exam; consider one ECG; iron studies if frequentEEG ± imaging as indicatedECG, Holter/event monitor, cardiology

Paroxysmal Kinesigenic Dyskinesia (PKD)

Age: typically school‑age through adolescence.

Phenomenology: brief attacks (seconds to <1 minute) of dystonia, chorea, or mixed dyskinesia (often unilateral or asymmetric); described as "twisting," "jerking," or "locking up."

Trigger: sudden voluntary movement or start (standing up quickly, taking first step, running, sudden direction change); often many attacks per day.

Awareness: preserved; no postictal state.

Between attacks: normal neurologic and developmental exam.

Course: often familial; commonly associated with PRRT2 variants; excellent response to low‑dose carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine.

Management:

  • Suspect with recurrent, stereotyped, movement‑triggered dyskinesias and normal EEG/MRI.
  • Consider trial of low‑dose carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine after routine labs/ECG and risk discussion.
  • Genetics referral if typical phenotype, strong family history, or additional features.

Primary Motor Stereotypies vs Tics

Stereotypies

  • Onset: infancy/early childhood (<3 years).
  • Movements: rhythmic, patterned, prolonged (e.g., hand flapping, rocking).
  • Triggers: excitement, engrossment; often when sitting or standing still.
  • Awareness: intact; distraction can interrupt behavior.
  • Course: long‑standing but non‑progressive.

Tics

  • Onset: usually >4–5 years.
  • Movements: brief, non‑rhythmic, can migrate (eye blinks, throat clears, shoulder shrugs, etc.).
  • Premonitory urge and relief after tic; suppression possible but uncomfortable.
  • Course: waxing and waning over weeks–months.

Sandifer Syndrome

Age: infancy/early childhood, often 2–18 months.

Phenomenology: episodic, often dramatic arching and dystonic posturing of neck and trunk (opisthotonus, torticollis‑like), commonly around feeds.

Association: gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and/or hiatal hernia; may have poor weight gain, irritability, or feeding aversion.

Features suggesting Sandifer rather than seizure:

  • Temporal relationship to feeding or supine positioning after feeds.
  • Child usually alert and interactive between spells.
  • Normal EEG.

Management: reflux measures (thickened feeds, positioning, acid suppression when appropriate); GI referral if poor growth or refractory symptoms.

Benign Paroxysmal Vertigo of Childhood (BPVC)

Age: typically 2–6 years.

Phenomenology: sudden episodes of vertigo/unsteadiness; child may grab caregiver, appear frightened, or fall; may have nystagmus, pallor, vomiting.

Duration: minutes, then complete recovery.

Association: strong personal/family history of migraine; often evolves into migraine later.

Work‑up: normal neurologic exam between attacks; if atypical or persistent ataxia, perform MRI ± vestibular evaluation.

Management: reassurance; treat as migraine variant if frequent (lifestyle, prophylaxis).

Benign Paroxysmal Torticollis of Infancy

Age: onset usually 3–8 months, resolving by 3–5 years.

Phenomenology: recurrent episodes of head tilt (often alternating sides) with pallor, irritability, vomiting, or ataxia.

Duration: hours to days; normal baseline between episodes.

Association: migraine spectrum; many later develop migraine.

Work‑up: neurologic and ocular exam; imaging if baseline torticollis persists, there are focal deficits, or course is atypical.

Management: reassurance; treat associated conditions (reflux, migraine triggers); PT if persistent neck tightness.

Spasmus Nutans

Age: typically 4–18 months.

Triad:

  • Fine, high‑frequency (often asymmetric) nystagmus.
  • Head nodding.
  • Intermittent torticollis.

Course: generally improves and resolves by age 3–4 years.

Critical consideration: optic pathway or other intracranial lesions can mimic this presentation.

Work‑up: detailed ophthalmologic exam; MRI brain and orbits with contrast if atypical age, visual symptoms, optic disc abnormalities, or persistent/progressive signs.

Management: if imaging and ophthalmology are normal, reassure and monitor.

Self‑Gratification Behaviors (Infantile Masturbation)

Age: approximately 6 months–5 years.

Phenomenology: episodes of quiet staring or grunting with rhythmic pelvic or thigh adduction, leg stiffening, or rubbing against surfaces; often facial flushing, sweating.

Clues:

  • Characteristic posture (prone, or sitting with tightly adducted legs).
  • Awareness preserved; episodes can be interrupted or distracted, but child may appear frustrated when interrupted.
  • No postictal state; occur in specific settings (car seat, crib, high chair).

Management: explain benign nature and non‑epileptic mechanism; reassure caregivers; avoid shaming; encourage gentle redirection; no ASM or imaging.

Night Terrors / Confusional Arousals vs Nocturnal Seizures

Night terrors / confusional arousals

  • Age: typically 3–10 years.
  • Timing: first third of the night, from N3 sleep.
  • Phenomenology: sudden screaming, autonomic activation (tachycardia, sweating), thrashing; child is inconsolable, appears confused, does not recognize caregivers.
  • Duration: minutes; resolves with return to sleep.
  • Morning: amnesia for the event.
  • Management: sleep hygiene, scheduled awakenings if frequent; consider sleep study when atypical.

Nocturnal seizures

  • Timing: any sleep stage; may cluster around sleep onset or early morning.
  • Phenomenology: stereotyped tonic, focal motor, or hypermotor events; eye deviation; cyanosis; tongue biting; postictal confusion or sleepiness.
  • Work‑up: video‑EEG monitoring and MRI as appropriate.
  • Management: standard epilepsy evaluation and treatment.

Non‑Epileptic Paroxysms – Summary Table

Entity Typical age Trigger Awareness Duration Key clues Basic work‑up Management
Primary motor stereotypies6–24 mo (often persist)Excitement, anticipation, engrossment, feeding, seatsNormalSeconds, clustersHighly patterned, same movement each time; distractibleNone if classic; consider developmental screenReassure; video capture; behavioral strategies if needed
Shuddering attacks4–12 moExcitement, feeding, spontaneousNormal1–2 s, many/dayVery brief "shiver" of trunk/head; no postictalNone if typicalReassure; no ASM/EEG
Breath‑holding spells6–18 moCrying, pain, fearTransient LOC with rapid recovery<30–60 sCyanotic (anger) or pallid (pain/fright); stereotyped sequenceConsider ECG once; CBC/ferritinReassure; safety; treat iron deficiency
Sandifer syndrome2–18 moDuring/after feedsNormalSeconds–minutes; cluster around feedsArching/twisting with GERD signs or poor weight gainGI evaluation as indicatedReflux therapy; feeding changes; GI referral
Benign paroxysmal vertigo2–6 ySpontaneous, position changeNormal but frightenedMinutesVertigo/unsteadiness ± nystagmus/vomiting; migraine family history; normal betweenNeuro exam; MRI if atypicalReassure; migraine‑variant management if frequent
Benign paroxysmal torticollis3–8 mo onsetSpontaneous; sometimes post‑viralNormalHours–daysRecurrent head tilt, alternating sides; pallor/emesis; well betweenEye exam; MRI if atypical/persistentReassure; consider migraine link; PT if baseline tightness
Spasmus nutans4–18 moNone specificNormalChronic course; individual movements briefTriad: head nodding, fine nystagmus, intermittent torticollisOphthalmology + MRI brain/orbitsIf work‑up normal: reassure; monitor
Self‑gratification6 mo–5 yBoredom, drowsiness, car seat, cribNormalSeconds–minutesPelvic/leg adduction, rubbing, flushing; stops with distractionNone if classic; video helpfulReassure; normalize; gentle redirection
PKDSchool‑age–teenSudden movement/startNormalSecondsMovement‑triggered dystonia/chorea; many/day; normal betweenEEG/MRI often normal; consider geneticsLow‑dose carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine; follow‑up
Night terrors / confusional arousals3–10 yFirst third of nightConfused, unresponsive during eventMinutesScreaming, autonomic signs, amnesia; non‑stereotyped motor patternSleep history ± PSG if atypicalSleep hygiene; scheduled awakenings if frequent
Nocturnal seizuresAnySleep transitions or any stageAltered during; postictalSeconds–minutesStereotyped tonic/focal/hypermotor; tongue bite; postictalVideo‑EEG; MRIEpilepsy work‑up and treatment

Neonatal Jitteriness vs Seizures

One of the most common consult questions in the nursery/NICU. Key clinical features distinguish jitteriness (benign) from seizures (requires workup).

Feature Jitteriness Seizure
Movement typeTremor (equal amplitude, frequency in both directions)Clonic (fast phase in one direction, slow return)
Stimulus-sensitiveYes — provoked or worsened by stimulationNo — occurs spontaneously
Suppressible with flexion/restraintYes — stops when limb is gently heldNo — continues despite restraint
Eye deviationAbsentMay have tonic eye deviation or nystagmus
Autonomic changesAbsentMay have HR/BP changes, desaturations, apnea
Level of consciousnessAlert, normalMay be altered (obtunded, poorly responsive)
Dominant movementRhythmic, symmetric, fine/rapidRhythmic but may be asymmetric; can be subtle (lip smacking, eye fluttering, cycling)
EEG correlationNo epileptiform activityUsually has electrographic correlate (though some seizures are electrographic-only)

Common Causes of Neonatal Jitteriness

  • Hypoglycemia (check glucose!)
  • Hypocalcemia
  • Neonatal drug withdrawal (maternal SSRI, opioids)
  • Hypothermia
  • Sepsis (can also cause seizures — low threshold to work up)
  • Idiopathic / benign neonatal jitteriness (diagnosis of exclusion)

Seizure Mimics

Seizure Mimics in Adults

Studies indicate that approximately 20% of patients presenting for evaluation of seizure are misdiagnosed as having epilepsy. Conditions most commonly mistaken for epileptiform seizure activity include syncope and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures.

Syncope: Syncope is a LOC due to decreased cerebral perfusion, resulting in loss of postural tone, with rapid return to mental status baseline. Syncope may be cardiac, orthostatic, or neurocardiogenic (vasovagal) in origin.

Psychogenic Non-Epileptic Spell/Episode (PNES): PNES, a condition characterized by the presence of seizure-like activity occurring in the absence of EEG changes. Difficult to differentiate from an epilepstic seizure in the emergency setting. Up to 20-40% of patients with epilepsy suffer from the NES. Characteristics that make PNES more likely include:
